name: Hyperspace Jump (hard)
statement:
It is now 125 years later, but humanity is still on the run from a humanoid-cyborg race determined to destroy it. Or perhaps we are getting some stories mixed up here... In any case, the fleet is now smaller. However, in a recent upgrade, all the navigation systems have been outfitted with higher-dimensional, linear-algebraic jump processors.
Now, in order to make a jump, a ship's captain needs to specify a subspace of the d-dimensional space in which the events are taking place. She does so by providing a generating set of vectors for that subspace.
Princess Heidi has received such a set from the captain of each of m ships. Again, she would like to group up those ships whose hyperspace jump subspaces are equal. To do so, she wants to assign a group number between 1 and m to each of the ships, so that two ships have the same group number if and only if their corresponding subspaces are equal (even though they might be given using different sets of vectors).
Help Heidi!
input:
The first line of the input contains two space-separated integers m and d (2‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČm‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČ30‚ÄČ000, 1‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČd‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČ5) ‚Äď the number of ships and the dimension of the full underlying vector space, respectively. Next, the m subspaces are described, one after another. The i-th subspace, which corresponds to the i-th ship, is described as follows:
The first line contains one integer ki (1‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČki‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČd). Then ki lines follow, the j-th of them describing the j-th vector sent by the i-th ship. Each of the j lines consists of d space-separated integers aj, j‚ÄČ=‚ÄČ1,‚ÄČ...,‚ÄČd, that describe the vector ; it holds that |aj|‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČ250. The i-th subspace is the linear span of these ki vectors.
output: Output m space-separated integers g1,‚ÄČ...,‚ÄČgm, where  denotes the group number assigned to the i-th ship. That is, for any 1‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČi‚ÄČ<‚ÄČj‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČm, the following should hold: gi‚ÄČ=‚ÄČgj if and only if the i-th and the j-th subspaces are equal. In addition, the sequence (g1,‚ÄČg2,‚ÄČ...,‚ÄČgm) should be lexicographically minimal among all sequences with that property.
note:
In the sample testcase, the first and the last subspace are equal, subspaces 2 to 4 are equal, and subspaces 5 to 7 are equal.
Recall that two subspaces, one given as the span of vectors  and another given as the span of vectors , are equal if each vector vi can be written as a linear combination of vectors w1,‚ÄČ...,‚ÄČwk (that is, there exist coefficients  such that vi‚ÄČ=‚ÄČőĪ1w1‚ÄČ+‚ÄČ...‚ÄČ+‚ÄČőĪkwk) and, similarly, each vector wi can be written as a linear combination of vectors v1,‚ÄČ...,‚ÄČvn.
Recall that a sequence (g1,‚ÄČg2,‚ÄČ...,‚ÄČgm) is lexicographically smaller than a sequence (h1,‚ÄČh2,‚ÄČ...,‚ÄČhm) if there exists an index i, 1‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČi‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČm, such that gi‚ÄČ<‚ÄČhi and gj‚ÄČ=‚ÄČhj for all j‚ÄČ<‚ÄČi.
